---
title: Using the CLI
description: Use the resq-flow CLI for relay checks, log inspection, live log tailing, and scoped or global manual debug log emission from a terminal or agent workflow.
---

The `resq-flow` CLI is the headless interface for the product.

Use it when you want to:

- check whether the relay is reachable without opening the UI
- inspect recent flow logs from a terminal
- tail live flow logs while you replay traffic or debug a worker
- emit a quick structured debug log for local validation without wiring a full producer change first
- give an AI agent or script stable JSON or JSONL instead of browser state

## CLI at a glance

The CLI lives in the `cli/` package inside the main `resq-flow` repo.

At a high level, it is:

- built in TypeScript
- developed and built locally with Bun
- emitted as a Node-compatible executable
- connected to the same relay surfaces that power the product UI

That means the CLI is not a separate control plane or a second flow model.
It is a thin headless client over the existing `resq-flow` relay.

## How it fits into the stack

The CLI sits on top of the same local stack as the UI:

```text
producer -> Vector -> Victoria + resq-flow relay -> CLI / UI
```

For the MVP, the CLI talks to relay surfaces that already exist:

- `GET /health`
- `GET /health/ingest`
- `GET /capabilities`
- `GET /v1/history`
- `GET /ws`

The runtime code stays Node-compatible, and the live streaming path uses the `ws` package for WebSocket support outside the browser.

## Why resq-flow has a CLI

The UI is great for topology, inspectors, and visual flow understanding.
The CLI exists because some workflows are faster and safer in a terminal:

- terminal-first debugging over SSH, tmux, or local shells
- quick relay checks before you open the browser
- reproducible, machine-friendly output for scripts, CI, and agents
- headless flow support for flows that are still useful before they have a polished graph

For AI agents in particular, the CLI gives a narrow and deterministic surface:

- `status` answers whether the relay is up and whether ingest is active
- `logs list` gives bounded recent history for one flow or all scopes
- `logs tail` gives a live stream that agents can filter and consume incrementally
- `logs emit` lets an agent or developer write one explicit log event into the live relay path
- `--json` and `--jsonl` avoid scraping browser output

## Skills and agent workflows

If you are using Codex or another agent, the current skill split should be:

- `flow-cli-read`
  inspect flow logs with `status`, `logs list`, and `logs tail`
- `flow-cli-write`
  add or change logs for an existing flow, then validate them here
- `flow-cli-create`
  scaffold a brand-new flow before you start iterating on its logs

The CLI is the validation and manual-debug surface.
Runtime producer changes should still go through normal application telemetry, not through CLI calls from app code.

## Before you start

This guide assumes that you already completed [Local setup](/docs/build/local-setup) and have the `resq-flow` repo checked out locally.

The canonical executable name is `resq-flow`.

## Install the command locally

1. From the `resq-flow` repo root, build the CLI:

   ```bash
   make build-cli
   ```

2. Link it into your shell once so the `resq-flow` command is available locally:

   ```bash
   cd cli
   npm link
   ```

3. Confirm that the command responds:

   ```bash
   resq-flow --help
   ```

4. If you want to run the full CLI test suite, including integration tests:

   ```bash
   make test-cli
   ```

If you do not want to link the package into your shell, you can still use the built fallback entrypoint directly:

```bash
node cli/dist/index.js --help
```

## Start the relay

For CLI-only work, start just the relay:

```bash
make dev-relay
```

If you also want the browser experience running alongside the CLI, start the full app:

```bash
make dev
```

The default relay base URL is `http://localhost:4200`.

## Choose the right command

Use these commands for different kinds of questions:

- `resq-flow status`
  for relay reachability and ingest health
- `resq-flow logs list --flow <flow-id>`
  for recent history for one flow
- `resq-flow logs list --all`
  for recent history across both flow-scoped and global logs
- `resq-flow logs tail --flow <flow-id>`
  for live events for one flow
- `resq-flow logs tail --all`
  for live events across all scopes
- `resq-flow logs emit --flow <flow-id> ...`
  for a live manual debug log that belongs to a flow
- `resq-flow logs emit --global ...`
  for a live manual debug log that stays unscoped

The important distinction is:

- `logs tail` uses the live relay WebSocket stream
- `logs list` uses the history query path
- `logs emit` writes a `type: "log"` event into the live relay WebSocket path for manual debugging

That means replay or live traffic can update the UI in live mode and update `logs tail` immediately, while `logs list` only shows data that is available through the history backend.

Use `logs emit` for manual debugging, not as the normal producer-side logging path.

For the local fixture path, see [Replay fixture traffic](/docs/build/local-setup#replay-fixture-traffic).

## Scope model

The CLI uses explicit scope.

- a log belongs to a flow only if it explicitly declares that flow
- valid flow ownership signals are `attributes.flow_id` or relay-assigned `matched_flow_ids`
- if neither exists, the log is global
- global logs do not appear in a specific flow UI

The base command shapes are:

```bash
resq-flow status
resq-flow logs list --flow <flow-id>
resq-flow logs list --all
resq-flow logs tail --flow <flow-id>
resq-flow logs tail --all
resq-flow logs emit --flow <flow-id> --message "<text>"
resq-flow logs emit --global --message "<text>"
```

Flag rules:

- `logs list` and `logs tail` require exactly one of `--flow <flow-id>` or `--all`
- `logs emit` requires exactly one of `--flow <flow-id>` or `--global`

## Check relay status

Use `status` when you want the fastest answer to "is the relay up, and is it seeing traffic?"

```bash
resq-flow status
```

Use JSON output when a script or agent needs stable fields:

```bash
resq-flow status --json
```

Supported flags:

- `--url <base-url>`
- `--json`
- `--timeout <ms>`

Expected output:

- default output is a short human-readable summary for relay and ingest health
- `--json` returns stable machine-readable fields for scripts or agents

## List recent logs

Use `logs list` when you want a bounded recent history window.

Start with the default output:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline
resq-flow logs list --all
```

Narrow the window or result size:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --window 15m
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --limit 20
```

Switch to machine-friendly output when you need to pipe or parse results:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --json
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --jsonl
```

Supported flags:

- exactly one of `--flow <flow-id>` or `--all`
- `--window <duration>` where duration is `<number><unit>` and unit is `s`, `m`, or `h`
- `--attr <key=value>` repeatable
- `--query <text>`
- `--limit <n>`
- `--json`
- `--jsonl`
- `--url <base-url>`

Expected output:

- default output is a human-readable table
- flow-scoped rows show the flow id, run id, stage, status, and message
- `--all` can include `global` rows for unscoped logs
- `--json` returns one array
- `--jsonl` returns one JSON object per line

## Filter logs with attributes

Attribute filters are exact matches.
Use one or more `--attr key=value` flags to narrow to a specific run, stage, worker, or status.

Examples:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr thread_id=thread-201
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr run_id=thread-201
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr stage_id=send.provider_call
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr worker_name=mail_sender
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr status=error
```

You can combine filters:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --attr thread_id=thread-201 --attr status=error
```

The current `mail-pipeline` fixture and relay response shapes make these keys especially useful:

- `thread_id`
- `stage_id`
- `run_id`
- `flow_id`
- `function_name`
- `worker_name`
- `status`
- `component_id`

## Filter logs with text search

Use `--query` when you want substring matching across messages and other useful fields.

Examples:

```bash
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --query timeout
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --query draft
resq-flow logs list --flow mail-pipeline --query thread-201
```

`--query` is often useful when you do not know the exact attribute key yet.
Use `--attr` when you want precise, repeatable filtering.

## Tail live logs

Use `logs tail` when you want to watch matching flow logs arrive in real time.

Start the live stream:

```bash
resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline
resq-flow logs tail --all
```

Then in another terminal, replay or trigger matching traffic.

If you want the built-in fixture path, use [Replay fixture traffic](/docs/build/local-setup#replay-fixture-traffic).

Filter a live tail to one run or stage:

```bash
resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline --attr thread_id=thread-201
resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline --attr stage_id=send.provider_call
```

Filter by text:

```bash
resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline --query timeout
```

Use JSONL for scripts and AI agents:

```bash
resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline --jsonl
```

Supported flags:

- exactly one of `--flow <flow-id>` or `--all`
- `--attr <key=value>` repeatable
- `--query <text>`
- `--jsonl`
- `--url <base-url>`

Expected output:

- default output is a streaming human-readable line format
- flow-scoped tail shows stage, run, status, and message
- `--all` includes a scope label such as `mail-pipeline` or `global`
- `--jsonl` is the best option for scripts and agents

## Emit ad hoc logs

Use `logs emit` when you want to inject one quick structured log into the live relay stream.

This is especially useful when:

- you want to validate a stage or run label before making a deeper producer change
- you want a fast smoke test for `logs tail`
- you want an AI agent to create a narrow, inspectable debug signal under the existing contract

### Emit a flow-scoped log

Use `--flow <flow-id>` when the log should belong to a specific flow:

```bash
resq-flow logs emit \
  --flow mail-pipeline \
  --message "picked thread for analysis" \
  --attr run_id=thread-301 \
  --attr thread_id=thread-301 \
  --attr stage_id=analyze.decision \
  --attr status=ok
```

What happens:

- the CLI prints a short confirmation such as `Emitted flow log to mail-pipeline.`
- `resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline` should receive the log immediately
- the live flow logs UI for `mail-pipeline` should also receive it
- if you include mappable fields like `stage_id`, `component_id`, `function_name`, or `worker_name`, the existing flow UX can map it cleanly to the flow

### Emit a global log

Use `--global` when the log should stay unscoped:

```bash
resq-flow logs emit \
  --global \
  --message "debug checkpoint before oauth refresh" \
  --attr subsystem=mail-auth
```

What happens:

- the CLI prints `Emitted global log.`
- `resq-flow logs tail --all` should receive it immediately
- it does not appear in a specific flow UI because it is not flow-scoped

### Supported flags

- exactly one of `--flow <flow-id>` or `--global`
- `--message <text>` required
- `--attr <key=value>` repeatable
- `--url <base-url>`

### Recommended flow-scoped attributes

These are especially useful when you want the log to be easy to query or visually map:

- `run_id`
- `thread_id`
- `stage_id`
- `component_id`
- `function_name`
- `worker_name`
- `status`

### Important note about `logs list`

`logs emit` writes to the live relay path.

That means:

- emitted logs should show up in `logs tail` immediately
- emitted logs only show up later in `logs list` if your history backend persists and serves them through `/v1/history`

## Recommended workflow

Use the CLI when the problem is narrow and operational:

- `status` before deeper debugging
- `logs list` for bounded investigation of one flow, one run, or all scopes
- `logs tail` during replay, worker debugging, or live validation
- `logs emit` when you want to plant one explicit test signal and immediately verify it

Use the UI when the problem is visual or exploratory:

- understanding flow shape
- using inspectors and the graph together
- comparing multiple nodes or runs in context

In practice, the best workflow is often both:

1. use the CLI to confirm relay health or isolate one run
2. open the UI when you need graph context or deeper drill-down
